sections.markdown: # Intro to Grail and DQL - Part 2

## EXERCISE 4: DQL and NEW Smartscape (on Grail)

## Please duplicate the Notebook before editing it. If you need instructions, check out the Lab Guide. Thanks!

Let's take a look at the new Smartscape on Grail store. You can read up on the details after this session in the online documentation:
* https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/discover-dynatrace/platform/grail/smartscape-on-grail

## Lets explore the data
Let's now take a closer look at Smartscape on Grail data. In the exercises in this section we will cover the following concepts:
1. Loading nodes and edges
2. Analyzing configuration data of Kubernetes and Cloud resources
3. Working with tags and labels
4. Enriching and filtering timeseries
5. Working with Edges
6. Smartscape traversal

### Exercise 4.1 - Loading nodes and edges

You can use the `smartscapeNodes` and `smartscapeEdges` command to query nodes and edges. You need to specify the node types / edge types to query. Below are some examples:
* `smartscapeNodes "*"`
* `smartscapeNodes HOST`
* `smartscapeNodes "AWS*"`
* `smartscapeNodes {HOST, "K8S*"}`
* `smartscapeEdges "*"`
* `smartscapeEdges {runs_on, belongs_to}`

4.1.1 - How many Smartscape nodes exist?
* Query all `smartscapeNodes` using a wildcard of `*`
* `summarize` by `type` and `count()`
* `sort` by `count()` in `desc`ending order

input.value:
smartscapeNodes "*"
| summarize by:type, count()
| sort `count()` desc

grail.canonicalQuery:
smartscapeNodes "*"
| summarize by:{type}, count()
| sort `count()` desc
grail.query:
smartscapeNodes "*"
| summarize by:type, count()
| sort `count()` desc

notifications.messageFormat: The parameters should be grouped with curly braces: {}.

| summarize by:type, count()
| sort `count()` desc

sections.markdown:
4.1.2 - What types of edges exist?
* Query all `smartscapeEdges` using a wildcard
* `summarize` by `source_type`, `type` `target_type`, and get a `count()` of each combination

input.value:
smartscapeEdges "*"
| summarize by:{source_type, type, target_type}, count()

grail.canonicalQuery:
smartscapeEdges "*"
| summarize by:{source_type, type, target_type}, count()
grail.query:
smartscapeEdges "*"
| summarize by:{source_type, type, target_type}, count()

input.value:
smartscapeEdges "*"
| summarize by:{source_type, type, target_type}, count()

sections.markdown:
### Exercise 4.2 - Analyzing configuration data of Kubernetes and Cloud resources

Kubernetes and Cloud entities also use the Smartscape storage to store the underlying configuration the the resource in JSON format. In DQL you can parse the objects and extract data to support advanced use cases. Examples:
* `| parse k8s.object, "JSON:k8s.object"`
* `| parse aws.object, "JSON:aws.object"`

4.2.1 - Filter K8S Deployments with a resource version greater than 97953444
* Query `smartscapeNodes` of `K8S_DEPLOYMENT`
* `fieldsKeep` field `k8s.object` to reduce the dataset
* Use `parse` to parse the k8s.object field into a JSON variable named `k8s.object`
* Lastly, we want to `filter` for `k8s.object[metadata][resourceVersion]` greater than or equal to `97953444`

input.value:
smartscapeNodes K8S_DEPLOYMENT
| fieldsKeep k8s.object
| parse k8s.object, "JSON:k8s.object"
| filter toLong(k8s.object[metadata][resourceVersion])>=97953444

command: if curl -s "http://localhost:9090" -H "Connection: keep-alive" -H "User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/96.0.4664.110 Safari/537.36 OPR/82.0.4227.50" | grep -Eq "ruxitagentjs"; then echo "Agent found"; exit 0; else echo "Agent not found"; exit 1; fi

grail.canonicalQuery:
smartscapeNodes K8S_DEPLOYMENT
| fieldsKeep k8s.object
| parse k8s.object, "JSON:k8s.object"
| filter toLong(k8s.object[metadata][resourceVersion]) >= 97953444
grail.query:
smartscapeNodes K8S_DEPLOYMENT
| fieldsKeep k8s.object
| parse k8s.object, "JSON:k8s.object"
| filter toLong(k8s.object[metadata][resourceVersion])>=97953444

input.value:
smartscapeNodes K8S_DEPLOYMENT
| fieldsKeep k8s.object
| parse k8s.object, "JSON:k8s.object"
| filter toLong(k8s.object[metadata][resourceVersion])>=97953444

sections.markdown:
### Exercise 4.3 - Working with tags and labels

All Native Cloud tags and K8s labels and annotations will be added to the nodes as tags. You can access them via the nested fields `tags:<context>` - the context gives you an idea where those tags are coming from. If you need to use the context, make sure you backtick the field names, because they contain the `:`. If the context is not important for your use-case, you can access all tags via the field `tags`.

4.3.1  - Review all host tags

* Start by loading all `smartscapeNodes` of `HOST`
* Next, focus just on the `tags` field

input.value:
smartscapeNodes HOST
| fields tags

smartscapeNodes HOST
| fields tags

sections.markdown:
4.3.2 - Review the available tags fields of Kubernetes resources

* Start by loading all `smartscapeNodes` of `K8S_*`
* Next, focus just on the `tags` field
* Lastly, `filter` on just `tags:k8s.labels`[`app.kubernetes.io/managed-by`] equal to `Helm`

input.value:
smartscapeNodes "K8S_*"
| fields tags
| filter `tags:k8s.labels`[`app.kubernetes.io/managed-by`] == "Helm" 

// note the backticks// this gives the combined tags of the following 2 fields: `tags:k8s.labels`, `tags:k8s.annotations`

grail.query:
smartscapeNodes "K8S_*"
| fields tags // this gives the combined tags of the following 2 fields: `tags:k8s.labels`, `tags:k8s.annotations`

input.value:
smartscapeNodes "K8S_*"
| fields tags // this gives the combined tags of the following 2 fields: `tags:k8s.labels`, `tags:k8s.annotations`

sections.markdown:
### Exercise 4.4 - Analyzing timeseries data by Smartscape Identifier

Metrics, logs, events and other signal data contains Smartscape IDs as fields. They are all named `dt.smartscape.<type>`, very similar to the classic entity id fields that were named `dt.entity.<type>`. Currently, both fields exist on the data, referring to Smartscape and to the classic entity storage.

4.4.1 - Which metrics exist in your environment?

The `metric.series` table contains a list of all metric keys and their dimensions. We can use it to find all metric keys that refer to `HOST`, `SERVICE` or `AWS_EC2_INSTANCE`:
* Start with a `fetch` on `metric.series`
* Use `isNotNull` combined with the below field names to iterate thru each type (HOST, SERVICE, EC2)
* `summarize` by `metric.key` and `count()`

-------------
  
For HOSTs use dt.smartscape.host  
     For SERVICE use dt.smartscape.service  
     For AWS_EC2_INSTANCE use dt.smartscape.aws_ec2_instance

input.value:
fetch metric.series
| filter isNotNull(dt.smartscape.host)  // run again with dt.smartscape.service, and aws_ec2_instance   
| summarize by:metric.key, count()

sections.markdown:
4.4.2 - View Entity Identifier for NEW Smartscape and Classic Smartscape

In this example we will demonstrate that both NEW and Classic ID entity identifiers exist on the `timeseries` data

* Use `timeseries` on `avg(dt.service.request.count)` and do it by NEW Smartscape `dt.smartscape.service` and Classic `dt.entity.service` identifier fields

metrics.description: Number of requests received by a given service. To learn how Dynatrace detects and analyzes services, see [Services](https://dt-url.net/am-services).

sections.markdown:
4.4.3 - Enrich timeseries with Smartscape tags

Using the `getNodeField()` function allows you to look up Smartscape node metadata (like "tags") via Smartscape ID

* Use `timeseries` on `avg(container.cpu.time)` by `dt.smartscape.k8s_pod` 
* Use `fieldsAdd` to create a new field `k8s.labels` and set it eqal to function `getNodeField(dt.smartscape.k8s_pod, "tags")`
* Lastly, we want to `filter` for only when `k8s.labels[opentelemetry_community_demo]` is equal to `"true"`

grail.canonicalQuery:
timeseries by:{dt.smartscape.k8s_pod}, avg(container.cpu.time)
| fieldsAdd k8s.labels = getNodeField(dt.smartscape.k8s_pod, "tags")
| filter k8s.labels[opentelemetry_community_demo] == "true"

metrics.description: Total cumulative CPU time (sum of all cores) spent by the container/pod/node since its creation

sections.markdown: ### Exercise 4.5 - Working with Edges

4.5.1  Edges contain fields that are not visible by default so let's view them.

* Use `smartscapeEdge` with wild card * to view all edges
* Use `fieldsAdd` to add `source_type`, `target_type`, and `dt.system.edge_kind` to our data set

notifications.messageFormat: Your result has been limited to %1$s.
notifications.message: Your result has been limited to 1000.

notifications.messageFormat: Your result has been limited to %1$s.
notifications.message: Your result has been limited to 1000.

sections.markdown:
4.5.2 - Use function `getNodeName`to get the friendly names for fields `source_id` and `target_id` 

* Use `smartscapeEdge` with wild card * to view all edges
* Use `fieldsAdd` to create two new fields `source_name` and `target_name`, and leverage function `getNodeName` to get the friendly names for fields `source_id` and `target_id`

grail.canonicalQuery:
smartscapeEdges "*"
| fieldsAdd source_name = getNodeName(source_id), target_name = getNodeName(target_id)

sections.markdown:
## Exercise 4.6 - Smartscape traversal

Now lets take a look at the topology. The `traverse` command which allows you to move from a list of nodes, to related nodes.

4.6.1 - Use `traverse` command to Traverse from POD to NAMESPACE

* Use `smartscapeNodese` with `K8S_POD to view all POD edges
* `filter` on <NEED TO IDENTIFY>
* Use `fieldsKeep` just `name` and `id` to simplify the data set
* Lastly, we want to use `traverse` to identify the `K8S_NAMESPACE` that it `belongs_to`

input.value:
// typically you will start with a filtered list of pods
smartscapeNodes K8S_POD
| filter name == "frontend-5f8f88fc86-6kbcq"
| fieldsKeep name, id
| traverse belongs_to, K8S_NAMESPACE

grail.canonicalQuery:
smartscapeNodes K8S_POD
| filter name == "frontend-5f8f88fc86-6kbcq"
| fieldsKeep name, id
| traverse {belongs_to}, {K8S_NAMESPACE}
grail.query:
// typically you will start with a filtered list of pods
smartscapeNodes K8S_POD
| filter name == "frontend-5f8f88fc86-6kbcq"
| fieldsKeep name, id
| traverse belongs_to, K8S_NAMESPACE

sections.markdown:
4.6.2 - Use command `traverse "*", "*" ` to traverse multiple hops deep.  Let's practice with SERVICES

* Use `smartscapeNodes` to load Nodes of type `SERVICE`
* Use `filter` on field `name` where eqaul to `oteldemo.CheckoutService`
* Use `fieldsKeep` just `name` and `id` to simplify the data set
* Next, if we use `| traverse "*", "*" ` we have traversed one hop
* If we use `| traverse "*", "*" `, now we have traversed two hops
* To simply, let's `filter` on field `type` equal to `SERVICE` relationships

input.value:
// you can call traverse multiple times
// each hop will add an entry into the dt.traverse.history field, giving you the traversal path
smartscapeNodes SERVICE
| filter name == "oteldemo.CheckoutService"
| fieldsKeep name, id
| traverse "*", "*"  // 1 hop-level deep
| traverse "*", "*"  // 2 hop-levels deep
| filter type == "SERVICE" // focuses on just SERVICES relationships

sections.markdown:
4.6.3 - We can use the fieldsKeep parameter during traversal to preserve data along the way

* Let's start with the provided DQL below. Copy/Paste into the DQL box below
* Now, let's modify each `traverse` command line, adding ``, fieldsKeep: {name, type}`
* Lastly, to simplify, lets view only `fields` of `dt.traverse.history, id`, `name`, and, `type`


```
smartscapeNodes SERVICE
| filter name == "oteldemo.CheckoutService"
| fieldsKeep name, id
| traverse "*", "*"  
| traverse "*", "*" 
```

sections.markdown:
4.6.4 - Traverse Backward 

So far we have only been looking at **forward** relationships, but, we can also look backwards

* Let's start with the provided DQL below.  Copy/Paste it into the DQL box below


* Use `traverse "*", "*"` but now we want to add parameter `, direction:backward` to the end to indicate backward looking

```
smartscapeNodes SERVICE 
| filter name == "oteldemo.CheckoutService"
```

input.value:
smartscapeNodes SERVICE 
| filter name == "oteldemo.CheckoutService"
| traverse "*", "*", direction:backward

grail.canonicalQuery:
smartscapeNodes SERVICE
| filter name == "oteldemo.CheckoutService"
| traverse {"*"}, {"*"}, direction:backward
grail.query:
smartscapeNodes SERVICE 
| filter name == "oteldemo.CheckoutService"
| traverse "*", "*", direction:backward

sections.markdown: #### THIS IS GREAT STUFF BUT TOO MUCH CONTENT FOR TIME ALOTTED - I suggest we delete the below or move to "BONUS" material

sections.title: Find called services that are 1-3 hops away

input.value:
// traverse only gives you a single hop
// if you want to search for nodes that are several hops away, you can use the following pattern to append multiple traverses
smartscapeNodes SERVICE
| filter name == "frontend"
| traverse calls, SERVICE
| append [
smartscapeNodes SERVICE
| filter name == "frontend"
| traverse calls, SERVICE
| traverse calls, SERVICE
]
| append [
smartscapeNodes SERVICE
| filter name == "frontend"
| traverse calls, SERVICE
| traverse calls, SERVICE
| traverse calls, SERVICE
]
// there might exist several paths to the same entities, lets deduplicate and only keep the shortest paths
| fieldsAdd path_length = arraySize(dt.traverse.history)
| dedup id, sort:path_length asc
| fields path_length, dt.traverse.history, id

grail.query:
// traverse only gives you a single hop
// if you want to search for nodes that are several hops away, you can use the following pattern to append multiple traverses
smartscapeNodes SERVICE
| filter name == "frontend"
| traverse calls, SERVICE
| append [
smartscapeNodes SERVICE
| filter name == "frontend"
| traverse calls, SERVICE
| traverse calls, SERVICE
]
| append [
smartscapeNodes SERVICE
| filter name == "frontend"
| traverse calls, SERVICE
| traverse calls, SERVICE
| traverse calls, SERVICE
]
// there might exist several paths to the same entities, lets deduplicate and only keep the shortest paths
| fieldsAdd path_length = arraySize(dt.traverse.history)
| dedup id, sort:path_length asc
| fields path_length, dt.traverse.history, id

input.value:
// In a similar approach like the example above, we can also leverage PROCESS-calls->PROCESS edges to find out which PODs are talking to which PODs
smartscapeNodes K8S_POD
| traverse "*", CONTAINER, direction:backward, fieldsKeep:{type , name}
| traverse "*", PROCESS, direction:backward 
| traverse calls, PROCESS
| traverse "*", CONTAINER
| traverse "*", K8S_POD
| fields source_id = dt.traverse.history[0][id], type="calls", target_id = id

grail.query:
// In a similar approach like the example above, we can also leverage PROCESS-calls->PROCESS edges to find out which PODs are talking to which PODs
smartscapeNodes K8S_POD
| traverse "*", CONTAINER, direction:backward, fieldsKeep:{type , name}
| traverse "*", PROCESS, direction:backward 
| traverse calls, PROCESS
| traverse "*", CONTAINER
| traverse "*", K8S_POD
| fields source_id = dt.traverse.history[0][id], type="calls", target_id = id
